content: Node graph UX pitfalls: (1) Full graph rendered on first load overwhelms novice users — cognitive overload before they can orient. (2) No obvious entry point in a web of 50+ nodes — users do not know where to start. (3) Automatic layout algorithms cluster nodes by graph topology not by domain logic — a Fruchterman-Reingold layout may place 'scouting' far from 'map control' even though they are conceptually adjacent. (4) Edge/connection clutter is worse than node count — too many edges make the graph unreadable; selective edge display is essential. (5) Mobile pan/zoom experience on a touch device with many nodes is notoriously poor — pinch-zoom conflicts with scroll. (6) Color coding as the only differentiator fails for colorblind users and loses meaning when overused. (7) Search and filter are not optional above ~30 nodes — without them, users cannot navigate to specific concepts. (8) Progressive disclosure (show neighbors of selected node, hide rest) is the standard mitigation for all of the above.
